title: Linear Stability of Shrinking Ricci Solitons and First Eigenvalue Estimates
speaker: Huai-Dong Cao
abstract: Ricci solitons, introduced by R. Hamilton in the mid-80s, are self-similar solutions to the Ricci flow and natural generalizations of Einstein manifolds. Shrinking Ricci solitons, in particular,  model Type I singularities of the Ricci flow and arise as critical points of Perelman's ν-entropy. In this talk, after a quick introduction on (gradient) Ricci solitons, we shall discuss the linear stability of shrinking Ricci solitons with respect to Perelman's \nu-entropy and the first eigenvalue estimates of the Laplace–Beltrami/Lichnerowicz-type Laplacian operators.

proof_stages.label: Gradient Ricci soliton equation
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines gradient Ricci solitons, relates shrinkers/steady/expanders to Einstein metrics and general solitons, and writes the equation as a quasilinear second-order elliptic system.

proof_stages.label: Solitons as Ricci-flow singularity models
proof_stages.summary: Shrinking and steady solitons are motivated as self-similar Ricci-flow solutions arising from blow-ups of finite-time singularities, with type-I singularities yielding nonflat shrinking soliton limits.

proof_stages.label: Low-dimensional classification and four-dimensional controls
proof_stages.summary: Known classifications in dimensions two and three are reviewed, and four-dimensional shrinkers are shown to have strong potential, volume, scalar-curvature, and full-curvature controls that make rigidity arguments possible.

proof_stages.label: Positive-curvature and special-geometry rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Using the asymptotic estimates and weighted maximum-principle arguments, the talk explains compactness/splitting results under nonnegative sectional curvature and surveys rigidity under special hypotheses such as Weyl conditions and positive isotropic curvature.

proof_stages.label: Scarcity of nontrivial shrinker constructions
proof_stages.summary: The few known nonstandard shrinking solitons are described, emphasizing that four-dimensional examples are almost all Kähler and that non-Kähler shrinkers remain very scarce.

proof_stages.label: Perelman entropy and second variation
proof_stages.summary: Perelman's entropy functionals are introduced so that gradient shrinkers are critical points, and the second variation is expressed through a stability operator involving the Lichnerowicz Laplacian plus gauge and trace terms.

proof_stages.label: Eigenvalue criteria for Einstein stability
proof_stages.summary: For positive Einstein metrics the variation space is decomposed into gauge, conformal, and transverse-traceless parts, yielding stability precisely when the first scalar Laplace eigenvalue and the first Lichnerowicz eigenvalue meet the threshold twice the Einstein constant.

proof_stages.label: Known stability consequences and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The eigenvalue criteria and weighted Lichnerowicz calculations are applied to known Einstein and shrinking-soliton examples, leading to Hamilton's conjecture and open questions on compact stable shrinkers and first-eigenvalue bounds.

examples.name: Dumbbell neck-pinch singularities
examples.note: The dumbbell and its degenerate neck-pinch variant are used to show why parabolic blow-up distinguishes type-I shrinking-cylinder limits from subtler type-II steady limits, motivating the singularity-model viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker geometrically analyzes the dumbbell neck pinch, explaining the shrinking S2 neck, finite-time curvature blow-up, parabolic dilation, and cylinder/Bryant-soliton limits.

passes.reason: Used as a standard motivating Ricci-flow finite-time singularity modeled by a shrinking cylinder, not as a refutation or structurally sporadic/extreme case.
a standard motivating type-I Ricci-flow neck-pinch on a dumbbell, used to illustrate finite-time singularity formation rather than refute a claim

A dumbbell neck-pinch is used as a standard motivating Ricci-flow singularity whose neck limits to a shrinking cylinder, not as a refutation or a structurally sporadic/maximally symmetric/extremal case.
a standard generic Ricci-flow neck-pinch is used as a motivating model of finite-time singularity formation, not to refute a claim

Used as a standard motivating Ricci-flow finite-time singularity model rather than to refute a claim, and the ordinary dumbbell neck-pinch is not intrinsically sporadic, maximally symmetric, collapsed, or optimal.
standard dumbbell neck-pinches are used as motivating finite-time Ricci-flow singularities modeled by cylinders or solitons, not as sporadic, extremal, maximally symmetric, or refuting examples

Used as a standard motivating example of finite-time Ricci-flow singularity formation rather than to refute a claim; a generic dumbbell neck-pinch is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, maximally symmetric, or sporadic.
Used as a motivating Ricci-flow singularity rather than a disproof; its neck collapses in a finite-time limiting case, with the standard dumbbell neck-pinch giving a tight Type-I blow-up model.

Used as a standard motivating Ricci-flow singularity model whose blow-up tends to a cylinder/Bryant-type soliton, not to refute a claim, and it is not intrinsically maximally symmetric, sporadic, degenerate, or extremal.
Used as a motivating Ricci-flow singularity, with a neck collapsing at finite time and the ordinary dumbbell case realizing the sharp Type-I blow-up rate.

Used as a standard motivating example of finite-time Ricci-flow singularity formation, specifically a generic type-I neck pinch, not as a counterexample and not structurally sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.

It is used as a motivating illustration of a generic finite-time Ricci-flow neck-pinch modeled by a shrinking cylinder, not to refute a claim, and it is not a sporadic, minimal, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
